title: Living in Boulder vlog 12 - 8 - 18: Smokin weed & riding 100s indoors making me retarded

transcript.text: Hello, living in Boulder, V-Log, 8.29 p.m. on December 8th.
I'm going to do my bike ride real quick, just after I do the video here.
I don't have much to do to get ready, just put my contacts on, I think.
My brain's not working so good, so I don't have a whole lot, I can't think of a whole lot to say.
Suddenly riding my bike all the time, or six hours a day, every day is like, kind of feels like sucking all the blood out of my brain, you know.
But that's cool, it's kind of like giving an oil change, you know.
It's like fasting, if you empty it out and then you fill it with emptiness, and then you can fill it back up with new things, new types of thoughts.
So, yeah, I was having a whole jolly old time last night riding my bike for six hours and eight minutes, 100 miles on the rollers.
I guess that's an average of 110 watts or something.
That's not much, man.
I've got a long way to go, but at least I can do 100 miles in six hours.
The day before was my record at 5.56, and then yesterday was 6.08.
Today, if I had to guess what I would do today, I'm going to guess around 6.10.
It's going to be under 6.30.
I, you know, I'm in diesel mode.
I'm a diesel rider.
I'm just consistent every day, same thing.
But it's interesting.
I like doing 100 miles every day consistently, same exact routine, because then I can, it's easier for me to monitor all the other fluctuations in my body.
Because I'm getting stronger, my legs are getting used, the muscles are building up, the tendons are building up, and everything.
And so I'm getting stronger.
My body's becoming more efficient with riding, so that's helping me with ride faster.
But at the same time, I'm like wearing out, wearing down, you know, like I need a rest.
So, it's like these two opposing forces, the, what do you call it, the dichotomy.
So, you know, we'll just see, like, which one.
I'm kind of thinking that, see, because all I did was sleep.
All I do, I get done riding, I kind of lay there having some time a little bit, and then I sleep, and then I get up and I eat.
So, I'm kind of thinking that my leg strength is going to pick up more.
Or, I guess this is my leg strength one.
My leg strength is going to pick up more than my, than my general tiredness is going to go down.
So, my general tiredness is going to go down like that, leg strength, and the tiredness is going to bounce back.
And then I'm going to do like this without having to take any days off.
My mom says I have to take Christmas off because we're going to go and do Christmas meals and presents and movie.
That's the routine.
So, um, but then, yeah, after that, and then I'll just do it until I get 30 days of it, 3,000 miles.
So, I'll have to do 31 days because I took a day off for Christmas, but, uh, or maybe I'll throw in the days.
No, I want to do it all with hundreds.
I did a couple, a few rides that were under 100 miles.
It's like 60, 65, and 60.
But, yeah, from the 100, I want to just do it solid until 3,000, and then, uh, and then that's when I'm going to go outside and do an actual 20K time trial and all the other distances.
The 200 meters, 500 meters, 1 kilometer, 2 kilometers, 3 kilometers, 4 kilometers, maybe even 5 kilometers, 10 kilometers, maybe even 15, 20 kilometers and 40 kilometers.
Oh, yeah, all the way up to 100 and do 100-mile time trial because by the time I'm done, um, by the time I'm done riding, um, all these hundreds of miles, I'll get it into my system.
It'll be my routine.
It'll be normal.
And so, to do the same thing outside will be the same.
I think I'll just, um, and I'm curious to see how fast it'll be.
I think I'll be faster.
I mean, I feel like I'm hammering, dude, and I'm only going, like, 72 miles an hour, I think.
I don't know, but maybe not.
But, um, I think these things are set for people my way for, like, 200.
I should have done the test to compare how fast it is in real life compared to this before I started, but I'll do it after.
But, um, but, yeah, just ride and then do a loop over and over again.
I'm going to do the haystack loop over and over again and then have, uh, water bottles just hidden in the grass at the top of the climb on Highway 36 there when you, or after you connect from Neva.
So I can just, when I need the water, maybe just grab water once, because I think I go through 100 miles with four water bottles.
So bring two water bottles with me and then just, yeah, one day, and then one lap, just come up and just throw the water bottles down and grab the other ones.
Um, or else, or else just do it with four water bottles.
Um, yeah, or maybe do it both ways.
And then, of course, after that, the 200-mile time trial.
And then there's the 12-hour that I would, I would want to progress it all the way up.
But whatever, I mean, there's so much that that's the, uh, that's the beauty of it.
You just, you just make a routine that that's what you do every day, and then you just choose what you want to do based on, um, what your body's capable of.
Um, yeah, so it's fun listening to music, um, uh, that's all I was doing, the music videos and stuff.
And the time just flies by, um, I haven't graduated to start thinking about other things yet, you know, like audiobooks or anything like that.
I'm even a movie, haven't had a movie.
Um, so we'll see.
But yeah, I'm going to take it easy today.
We'll probably, I predict today is going to be about 6.20.
Alright, peace out.
